proposition: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion.

supporting_language: (1) Supports: "Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion." (Simpson citing Angelini and Heskett; trial court dismissal affirmed)
assessment: Green - Case directly supports the proposition. Simpson affirmed dismissal of civil extortion claims, citing this exact principle.

proposition: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion.

proposition: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.
cited_case: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009)
retrieved_case: Ashcroft v. Iqbal (2009-05-18) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "To survive a motion to dismiss, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to 'state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.'" (quoting Twombly, 550 U.S. at 570)
assessment: Green - Exact language appears in the opinion at the cited page.

proposition: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.
cited_case: Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007)
retrieved_case: Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly (2007-05-21) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "[W]e do not require heightened fact pleading of specifics, but only enough facts to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face." (at 570)
assessment: Green - Exact language appears in the opinion at the cited page.

proposition: Courts disregard legal conclusions couched as factual allegations.
cited_case: Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 678 (2009)
retrieved_case: Ashcroft v. Iqbal (2009-05-18) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "Threadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice." (2) Supports: "'[L]abels and conclusions' or 'a formulaic recitation of the elements of a cause of action will not do.'" (quoting Twombly)
assessment: Green - The proposition is a standard paraphrase of Iqbal's holding. The opinion supports this at the cited page.

proposition: Communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged.

proposition: Communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged.

proposition: No Legitimate Claim or Privilege -- demands made without a good-faith basis are unprotected.
cited_case: Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co., 72 Ohio St.3d 415, 419 (1995)
retrieved_case: Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co. (1995-07-05) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Addresses different issue: The Kenty case involves insurance disputes -- whether an insurer (Transamerica) conspired to overcharge a customer for collateral protection insurance. The case discusses "good faith" in the context of the insurer's duty of good faith and fair dealing, not in the context of demand letters, extortion, or litigation privilege.
assessment: Red - The citation does not support the proposition. Kenty is an insurance case about civil conspiracy and breach of good faith in insurance contracts. It does not address extortion demands, settlement privilege, or the concept that demands without a good-faith basis are unprotected in the extortion context.

proposition: Mere "hard bargaining," even when the consequences are severe, is not extortion where claims are legitimate and conduct threatened is lawful.
cited_case: United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d 1198, 1205 (11th Cir. 2002)
retrieved_case: United States v. James Scott Pendergraft (2002-07-16) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: Court reversed Hobbs Act extortion conviction, holding that "their threat was neither 'wrongful' within the meaning of the Hobbs Act nor a 'scheme to defraud' within the meaning of the mail-fraud statute." (2) Supports: Court held that a threat to file a lawsuit, even if made in bad faith, is not "wrongful" under the Hobbs Act. Several courts cited for this proposition.
assessment: Green - The case reversed extortion convictions for threats to file a lawsuit, directly supporting the proposition that legitimate demands and hard bargaining don't constitute extortion.

proposition: "Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat."
cited_case: United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55, 70 (2d Cir. 1999)
retrieved_case: United States v. Autumn Jackson, Boris Sabas, Also Known as Boris Shmulevich, and Jose Medina, Also Known as Yosi Medina (1999-06-09) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Addresses different issue: The Jackson case involves criminal extortion prosecution of Autumn Jackson for threatening to disclose Bill Cosby's paternity and demanding payment. The court upheld the extortion conviction. The specific quoted language ("Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities... does not constitute an unlawful threat") was not found in the opinion text.
assessment: Red - The quoted language does not appear in the opinion. The Jackson case found that the defendants' conduct WAS extortion. The case does not support the proposition that reporting misconduct to authorities is not an unlawful threat -- it addresses a different type of threat (exposure of personal information for payment).

proposition: "Seeking a monetary settlement tied directly to legal claims does not become extortion merely because regulatory reporting is also contemplated."
cited_case: Flatley v. Mauro, 39 Cal.4th 299, 331 (2006)
retrieved_case: Flatley v. Mauro (2006-07-27) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Contradicts: The California Supreme Court held that attorney Mauro's demand letter constituted criminal extortion as a matter of law. The court found that "extortionate speech is not constitutionally protected" and Mauro's anti-SLAPP motion was properly denied. The specific quoted language about settlement demands and regulatory reporting does not appear in the opinion.
assessment: Red - The quoted proposition does not appear in the opinion. The Flatley court's actual holding is that the attorney's demand letter -- which included threats of regulatory reporting and media exposure -- DID constitute extortion as a matter of law. The brief's citation misrepresents the case holding.

proposition: "Where claims are based on enforceable legal rights, settlement demands are privileged."

retrieved_case: Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co. (1995-07-05) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Addresses different issue: Same as above -- Kenty is an insurance case about collateral protection insurance overcharges and civil conspiracy. No discussion of settlement demands, litigation privilege, or enforceable legal rights in the extortion/demand context.
assessment: Red - The citation does not support the proposition. Kenty does not address whether settlement demands based on enforceable legal rights are privileged.

proposition: "Advising of possible consequences from lawful reporting cannot form the basis of an extortion claim."
cited_case: United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d at 1205 (11th Cir. 2002)
retrieved_case: United States v. James Scott Pendergraft (2002-07-16) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: Court reversed extortion conviction where defendants threatened to file a lawsuit and use evidence in support. Held the threat was not "wrongful" under the Hobbs Act. The general principle that lawful threats (including advising of legal consequences) cannot support extortion charges is supported by the holding.
assessment: Green - The case's reversal of extortion convictions for threats to pursue legal action supports this proposition, even though the exact quoted language may be a paraphrase rather than a direct quote.

proposition: Ohio's public policy protections support whistleblower reporting activity.

retrieved_case: Kulch v. Structural Fibers, Inc. (1997-04-16) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: The Ohio Supreme Court held that an employee "who is discharged or disciplined for filing a complaint with the Occupational Safety and Health Administration concerning matters of health and safety in the workplace is entitled to maintain a common-law tort action against the employer for wrongful discharge in violation of public policy."
assessment: Green - Case directly supports Ohio's recognition of public policy protections for whistleblower/reporting activity.

proposition: The Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act displaces overlapping torts based on the same nucleus of facts.

retrieved_case: Stolle Machinery Company, LLC v. Ram Precision Industries (2015-03-16) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "The OUTSA incorporates the Uniform Trade Secrets Act's displacement of 'conflicting tort, restitutionary, and other laws of this state providing civil remedies for misappropriation.'" Discusses the scope of OUTSA preemption, citing Office Depot and other cases.
assessment: Green - Case directly supports the proposition that OUTSA displaces overlapping tort claims based on the same facts.

proposition: "Exceeds authorized access" is construed narrowly to cover obtaining information from areas to which a person lacks access privileges, not misuse of information obtained with authorized credentials.
cited_case: Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021)
retrieved_case: Van Buren v. United States (2021-06-03) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "An individual 'exceeds authorized access' when he accesses a computer with authorization but then obtains information located in particular areas of the computer -- such as files, folders, or databases -- that are off limits to him." The Court construed the CFAA narrowly, rejecting the government's broader interpretation.
assessment: Green - Case directly and exactly supports the proposition. This is the central holding of Van Buren at the cited pages.

proposition: The Sixth Circuit applies the same narrow Van Buren rule to CFAA claims.
cited_case: Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020)
retrieved_case: Royal Truck & Trailer Sales v. Mike Kraft (2020-09-09) - Ready
supporting_language: (1) Supports: The Sixth Circuit analyzes "exceeds authorized access" using definitional provisions of the CFAA and concludes it targets those who gain unauthorized access, not those who misuse authorized access. Consistent with Van Buren's narrow construction.
assessment: Green - Case applies the same narrow CFAA interpretation. Note: Royal Truck (2020) preceded Van Buren (2021), so the Sixth Circuit independently reached the same conclusion rather than 'applying' Van Buren.
